item.title: Not as Anglo-dependent as you'd suppose
item.content:encoded: The languages of Sweden, Denmark, and Norway all use vowel #3 as a one-letter preposed term — used very commonly to express subject enclosure, and therefore necessary for SCP records. Furthermore, Denmark's language also uses vowel #3 as a second-person plural pronoun — so that the Northern branch would not have an easy job when two or more personnel put mother-tongue research talks on paper. (My own name would get the black box, as that name has vowel #3 at the start!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More than a few years late, but thought someone needed to say:

Eye see what you've done there llama. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps Jaden Smith was actually onto something, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact occurred to me that "Twenty Ten", "Two Thousand and Ten" and "Two Zero One Zero" do not have the frowned upon character represented. You have my applause should the number have been purposefully chosen for that property.

item.title: Re: Grammar Error
item.content:encoded: You are incorrect. Singular they has been in use for hundreds of years in both fiction and documentation. Here is the Wikipedia article about it and here is the Merriam-Webster definition which includes examples of singular usage.

item.title: Grammar Error
item.content:encoded:
"The subject drops SCP-2010 once the song starts to play and always falls over near the speakers. They do not move from that spot unless the song has ended. "

"They" at the start of the second sentence is grammatically incorrect. Because you are referring to one person, use a singular third person pronoun, such as "he/she" (although I do acknowledge most people find that phrasing awkward) or "it." You could also just say "The subject" again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and discuss to comments

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple and short yet aesthetically pleasing. But, at the same time, I can't urge myself to upvote, but I do not dislike it enough for a downvote.

Also, the source code bit is interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found the secret in the source code, which was pretty cool.

Other than that: I commend you for making a viable SCP without the letter I, I really do. But I'm judging it as a normal SCP, and thus downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagined someone experiencing this then playing Zelda games and not being able to find bosses' weak spots

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Managing to write last passage with only four vowels - splendid, I give you that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh dang! 'eye' found the Easter egg *WINK WINK*

+ EYE am subtle
- not really

It's in the source code

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, but well… just not my appetite, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Myself, gave a down-vote and came to these comments. When what you had attempted was seen, thought about no-vote or even up-vote. But regardless your great war on the rogue vowel, SCP-2010 comes too jokey. So … down-vote and kudos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a while to realize the gimmick. I quickly +1'd afterwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First look: This is weird. Neutral vote…

Second look (after viewing comments): Wait, so there's no "i" at all. By God, it's like the antithesis of SCP-426. Okay, I can upvote to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh lord, I need to be more careful with my edits. Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 0_0 The meta is broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I, uhhh, hate to break it to everyone, but…

SCP-2010 should be kept at Site-242 on the second shelf of secure storage vault four unless tests are needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A first I thought this was a n00b coldpost and downvoted, wondering why the rating was so high. I then came here, went and upvoted, and have returned to pay tribute to the grand master of writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thInk It could be rewrItten do be some halfway decent -j.

but It just does not cut It to be a real skIp.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
I down-voted the article, then I read this comment and in view of the fact that you were writing it with one arm behind your back (so to speak) I up-voted it.

True story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Apple Computer" could work too, as that was their name before they started making MP3 players.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On one hand, it's a really clever idea, but I just don't think the effect is explained very well. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes yes yes thousand synonym for multaplacon me would love to see that

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded: The rating module is a default thing, as far as I know. I'm not entirely sure if there's a way to customize it for specific pages.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded: Can you get a staff member to change the rating module to score?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with the above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the gimmick, but past that, it doesn't really make sense. If listening to the track makes you lose the concept of eyes, why is the article written without I's? And wouldn't the O5 or whoever is in charge of making sure these documentations are of acceptable quality notice the complete lack of I's and get someone else to write it?
No vote for now, as it doesn't really make sense. I do commend you for completing the challenge, though.
EDIT: I mean, I guess what the author was trying to get across was that the Order of the Four Vowels succeeded and the letter I was successfully removed from our alphabet, but I still think this is too gimmicky and disruptive to the Foundationverse for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's why every word has that letter. Every word that's part of your comment.
That took too long to type up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am sure I am not the only one to "control F" this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right, and I have now made it The Order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They should not refer to themselves as a cult, as that lessens the regard they hold for themselves. Perhaps Temple or Order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. Eyes. Is. Ha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I missing something here? Ah, I get it. After I read the author's notes anyway, good job on how you handled the challenge! But with it being an actual mainlist SCP, I'm going to have to judge it as such.

Sure, listening to the song will erase the concept of "I" from your mind, but why does the form itself not contain I's? I take it that the researcher assigned to this one listened to it himself; but if this is the case, couldn't they have gotten another researcher to write the report?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't sure to vote until after I saw your comment. I'm upvoting because this is a really hard thing to pull off, and I think it was done well. There are a few places that I think could be reworded while still keeping the gimmick, but I don't think it's bad enough to warrant a change in vote, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is cheesy and silly but the endearing kind of cheesy and silly. Can we have a tale about the Cult of the Four Vowels?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They do not move from that spot unless the song has ended.

I think you meant "until"?
J/K
Seriously though, "before" would read smoother I think.
Also, why is "four" spelled out in the containment procedures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's certainly no A Void, but I like it well enough. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would be amused if you somehow managed to make the word "rating" change to "score."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding. I feel like there was a lot more that could have been done with this and it just doesn't do anything for me as-is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I view stuff like this in the same way as I do any other kind of format screw. It can be a tool to do something different and worthwhile if done correctly. I ask myself whether the deviation in question is adding anything to the reading experience, if it's taking anything away, and if on balance I enjoyed the article more because of it.

In this case, the gimmick is at least thematically tied to the underlying concept, so it avoids the immediate "weirdness for its own sake" pitfall. Unfortunately, I feel that the "writing without 'i's" thing harmed your article more than it added to it. Because of the limitations you placed on yourself, this reads in a stilted, awkward manner that doesn't really do anything except irritate me as I read. I also feel you got a little caught up in the gimmick itself, as I find the core concept kind of thin and underdeveloped.

Used as a complementary piece within a stronger unified narrative, I might have been more receptive. But I can't support this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dislike this idea with immense passion. This gimmick with avoiding using "I" is tired, it's stupid, it's inane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure! Don't get me wrong, the SCP amuses me. But not much past that. Glad you had fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like the concept is at cross-purposes with the execution. The idea of a homophonic bleed from the memetic effect is really cool, but since you can't highlight how the eye-part is English-centric, or how it might also disrupt recognizing the affirmative due to "aye," the gimmick ends up as a detraction.

Kudos on the writing quality, but as a scip it just doesn't do it for me.

No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as I'm seeing at the moment, it basically comes down to a writing gimmick with little or no substance behind it. Like the crossword, but not as extreme.

Surely it took effort, time, dedication to write, but overall, what was written was…not much at all.

E: Also, Crowley was a stupid fucking worthless hack* and references to him make me eyes roll so hard it hurts.

*This dose of venom is reserved for the mentioned individual, not this article or author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I had a lot of fun writing this, and that's the most important thing to me. I probably could've worked harder, but I love what I've done with it as it is right now. I respect what you've said, but won't be changing a thing about this for the time being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just about to downvote until I reached the addendum. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Get what you meant to do. Honest. Enjoy what you attempted for sure. But you could have worked harder to pull the concept off. The concept deserves more than three hundred words to truly be great. Shouldn't be too hard: look at Gadsby, half a hundred thousand words, sans the letter "e". And he couldn't use "the"! Plus you have some needlessly odd phrases such as "the Apple Company." Why not use "Apple Computers"? Sure, not an exactly correct term, but that way surely sounds less awkward. You could use "smart phone" to be more clear too, shouldn't be a huge tone problem. So currently, no +1.

(My comment may sound harsh, sorry. Had to keep to the purpose, no tangents. Granted, to compose text styled as so presents constant obstacles. That last sentence was probably the hardest.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm torn. On the one hand, I liked the gimmick when a similar thing was done in SCP-1207. On the other hand…it was done in SCP-1207. Not really interested in seeing two of them, I liked 1207 more, and this really isn't anything but the gimmick. Gotta downvote on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so weird, hammy, and off the wall in an endearing way that I can't help but upvote. Watching this form in chat was slightly disturbing, yet fascinating.

Goddamnit, Devereaux.

item.title: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
This was done because of challenge Rumetzen gave me to write an article without using the letter i.

Thanks to CryogenChaos, Rumetzen, Bryx, DrYucatan, Joreth, and Riemann for helping me write this.

Thanks to many of those people, Weizhong, and TwistedGears for reviewing it.

Can you find the super secret super creepy Easter Egg (It's hidden somehow)?
